htmlContent:
The part of your brain you probably never think about

I want to talk about something that sits at the very base of your skull. It's called the brainstem. And honestly, most people have no idea what it does. They know about the amygdala. They've heard of the prefrontal cortex. But the brainstem? It's like the quiet kid in the back of the class who's actually running the whole operation.

Here's the thing. When your cortex checks out, which happens more often than you think, the brainstem doesn't just keep going. It takes over. Completely. And it does this without asking your permission. Without consulting your conscious mind. Without giving you a heads up that you're about to react in ways that make zero sense to your thinking brain.

I've been there. You've been there. That moment when someone says something mildly critical and your whole body goes offline. Your throat tightens. Your vision narrows. Your hands go cold. And you're standing there thinking, "Why am I reacting like this? I know this person isn't dangerous. I know I'm safe." But knowing that doesn't change what your body is doing. Because your brainstem doesn't care what you know.

Let's get specific about what we're dealing with. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, spent decades mapping how the brainstem controls our autonomic nervous system. He showed that the vagus nerve, which runs from the brainstem down through your body, is constantly scanning for cues of safety or danger. This happens below conscious awareness. You don't decide to feel safe. Your brainstem decides for you.

Think about that for a second. Your sense of safety isn't a choice. It's a biological response that's happening in the oldest, most primitive part of your brain. The part we share with reptiles. The part that doesn't have language. The part that can't reason or negotiate or tell you to calm down.

Wild, right?

So when your cortex checks out, which happens during overwhelm, trauma activation, or even just chronic stress, the brainstem doesn't panic. It does its job. It runs the show. And its job is to keep you alive by any means necessary.

Why your thinking brain goes offline first

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, has written extensively about what happens when trauma overwhelms the nervous system. He describes a process where the higher brain functions literally shut down to allow the more primitive survival responses to take over. Your prefrontal cortex, the part that can plan, reflect, and regulate, goes dark. Not because it's weak. Because it's designed to do that in the face of threat.

I remember sitting in my therapist's office years ago, completely dissociated, and she asked me a simple question about what I was feeling. I couldn't answer. I literally could not find the words. My cortex had checked out. My brainstem was running the show. And my brainstem doesn't do language. It does freeze, flight, fight, and collapse.

This is why talk therapy alone often fails for complex trauma. You can't talk your way through something that your brainstem doesn't understand. You can't reason with a part of your nervous system that predates language by millions of years.

Stay with me here. This is important. The brainstem doesn't just take over during obvious threats. It takes over during subtle ones too. A tone of voice. A facial expression. A certain smell. Anything that your nervous system has learned to associate with danger, even if your conscious mind doesn't register it, can trigger a brainstem takeover.

And once that happens, your cortex is offline. You can't think your way back to regulation. You have to work with the body first. You have to address the brainstem directly.

What brainstem takeover actually feels like

I want you to notice something right now. Take a breath. Just one. And pay attention to what's happening in your body. Is there tension anywhere? Are you holding your breath slightly? Is your jaw tight? Your shoulders up by your ears?

If any of that is happening, your brainstem might be running the show more than you realize. Chronic tension patterns are often brainstem-level responses that never got to complete their cycle. Your nervous system is stuck in a state of partial activation, ready to respond to a threat that may or may not be coming.

Here's what brainstem takeover looks like in real life:

You're having a conversation with someone you trust. They say something that brushes against an old wound. Suddenly you feel foggy. Your thoughts scatter. You can't find the right words. Your voice sounds distant. You feel like you're watching yourself from outside your body. Your cortex just checked out. Your brainstem is running the show now.

Or maybe it looks different for you. Maybe you feel a surge of heat. Your heart pounds. Your muscles tense. You want to run but you're sitting still. Your brainstem has decided that the best survival strategy is mobilization, even though there's nowhere to go.

Or maybe you go completely numb. Your body feels heavy. Your limbs feel like lead. You can't move. Your brainstem has chosen the oldest survival response of all: freeze. Play dead. Wait for the threat to pass.

None of these are choices. They are brainstem reflexes. And they happen in milliseconds, way before your cortex can even register what's happening.

For those looking for gentle nervous system support, something worth trying is NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link). It's an amino acid that can help take the edge off without sedating you. It works by increasing GABA and calming the excitatory signals in your brain. Not a fix, but a support.

How to work with the brainstem instead of fighting it

I spent years trying to think my way out of trauma responses. I read every self-help book. I journaled. I meditated. I did affirmations. None of it worked because I was trying to use my cortex to override my brainstem. That's like trying to use a calculator to put out a fire. Wrong tool for the job.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She's a clinician who studied directly with Stephen Porges and translates polyvagal theory into actual practices. The book is full of exercises that target the brainstem directly through breath, movement, and sound. It's not about thinking your way out. It's about signaling safety to your nervous system through the body.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium is involved in over 300 biochemical reactions in your body, and glycinate form is particularly good for calming the nervous system. It supports GABA receptors and helps your brainstem settle down. Again, not a cure, but a support for a system that's been running on high alert for too long.

But supplements and books only go so far. The real work is learning to track your nervous system states. To notice when your brainstem is taking over. To not judge yourself for it. To understand that this is your body trying to protect you, even when the protection isn't needed anymore.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) by Peter Levine. This is the book where he lays out his understanding of how trauma lives in the body and how we can release it through somatic work. It's dense but worth it. Levine shows how the brainstem holds onto incomplete survival responses and how we can help them complete.

What recovery actually looks like from here

I'm not going to tell you that you can train your brainstem to stop reacting. That's not how it works. The brainstem is not a muscle you can strengthen with reps. It's an ancient survival system that will always prioritize safety over comfort.

But you can build capacity. You can learn to recognize when your cortex has checked out. You can develop practices that help your brainstem come back online more quickly. You can create environments and relationships that signal safety to your nervous system over and over again until it starts to trust that maybe, just maybe, you're safe now.

This takes time. A lot of time. Your brainstem didn't learn to be on high alert overnight. It learned through years of experience, often in childhood, that the world was not safe. Unlearning that pattern takes repeated, consistent experiences of safety. Not a weekend retreat. Not a single conversation. Not a supplement. Years of showing your nervous system that it can relax.

And even then, there will be moments when your cortex checks out. When your brainstem takes over. When you find yourself reacting in ways that make no sense to your thinking brain. That's not failure. That's being human. That's having a nervous system that's doing its job.

The goal isn't to never have brainstem takeovers. The goal is to have them less often, recover from them faster, and be kinder to yourself when they happen. The goal is to understand that your brainstem is not your enemy. It's the part of you that kept you alive through things you shouldn't have had to survive. It deserves gratitude, not blame.

Sit with that.
